clean_headline: Vacuum Cleaner Will Convince Your Dog the Aliens Have Landed
abstract: Come spring, vacuuming the house may suddenly become fun. You'll be able to eat bonbons while a rotund little robot does the work. In April, James Dyson, an industrial designer in Chippenham, England, will introduce his robotic vacuum cleaner, the DC06, to the international market, including the United States. The round robot, at 14 inches in diameter and 20 pounds, starts cleaning at the perimeter of a room, then spirals inward. It is equipped with 55 sensors to help it go around furniture and avoid stairs.

clean_headline: Hunt for Meteorites In Antarctica Enlists a Novel Recruit
abstract: Robot named Nomad, four-wheeled machine built by Carnegie Mellon University's Robotics Institute, is set up in Antarctica to search rock-strewn areas of ice that are known to harbor meteorites and pick out objects that fell from space among ordinary rocks ands stones; photos; drawing (M)

clean_headline: New F.A.A. Computers on the Job in Syracuse
abstract: New computer system for air traffic control--so far behind schedule that last year federal officials gave up predicting when it would be finished--has begun service in Syracuse; system, for use in airport towers and low-altitude radar centers, still runs too slowly when it tracks hundreds of planes, and is not ready for busy air traffic offices; it is called Standard Terminal Automation Replacement System (M)

clean_headline: COMPANY BRIEFS
abstract: GALILEO INTERNATIONAL INC., Rosemont, Ill., a travel information company, said it would buy Travel Automation Services Ltd., which operates Galileo's distribution arm in England, from British Airways P.L.C. for an undisclosed amount.    INTERNATIONAL SPECIALTY PRODUCTS INC., Wayne, N.J., a specialty chemicals maker, said it planned to seek to elect a majority of the board of Dexter Corp., Windsor Locks, Conn., which makes materials ranging from tea-bag paper to computer chip coatings, in its effort to take over the company.

clean_headline: Accompanying a Robot on an Antarctic Quest
abstract: Nomad, robot created at Robotics Institite at Carnegie Mellon University, is being used in Antarctica to search for meteorites in continent's glaciers; one theory says that meteorite from Mars that was discovered in 1984 bears evidence of primitive microscopic life; Internet site (www.bignsignal.net) reports on Nomad's progress; photo of Nomad (M)

clean_headline: Accompanying a Robot on an Antarctic Quest
abstract: News Watch: Accompanying a Robot on an Antarctic Quest

clean_headline: Online Revolution's Latest Twist: Job Interviews With a Computer
abstract: Dozens of big-name retailers, including Target and Macy's, are replacing paper applications and in-person interviews with computer kiosks in initial screening of job applicants; computer programs query prospective employees on job history and work habits; they typically delve into psychological tests that companies say can help match job skills and personalities with openings; employers say automation gives them edge in tight labor market; critics ask how computer program can rightly judge person, even in initial screening; privacy advocates say employers, and companies hired to administer kiosks, are creating vast databases of applicants' psychological profiles that could find larger audience than applicants expect; photo (M)

clean_headline: ROBOT POPULATION RISING
abstract: Annual report by United Nations Economic Commission for Europe shows worldwide orders for industrial robots rose 20 percent last year; says rise was driven by increasing automation in US and Canada, where orders rose 60 percent in 1999 over previous year; in Europe, orders were up 12 percent (S)

clean_headline: Footnotes
abstract:
1. In a Kidbot world, Patty Playpal is just so last century, but if you're old enough to remember that disconcertingly life-size plastic companion, you're also old enough to remember giant rollers (do frozen-juice cans ring a bell?), like the ones Christophe Saluzzo used to give Chandler Hecht's hair that extra lift. There was no star attitude on this set. During the time it took to do her hair, Chandler, 6, who that very morning had calmly reminded her mother, Ellen, that ''this is my first print assignment,'' read (a) ''Sayonara, Mrs. Kackleman'' (Barnes & Noble, $17). Shawn Huang (b) also submitted cheerfully to the indignities of rollers -- which is a lot to ask of anyone, especially a 5-year-old guy.

2. All models were required to do two sittings in front of the camera -- one as themselves and one in metallic paint for their ''robot.'' Ryan Dozier (a), also on his first print job, thought the assignment was ''pretty interesting,'' reported his mother, Paulette McGee. But he was intent on making sure that all of the metallic paint was off him before the end of the day, fearing his brothers would tease him. Would that he had had (b) Shower Blast GeLather in Orange Missile with real fruit juice that ''explodes'' in lather (www.body-at-best.com, $6).

3. The robot heads were created by Todd Tucker of Wonderworld Entertainment. Tucker has created makeup for movies like ''The Mask,'' ''Mrs. Doubtfire'' and ''Dracula.'' Michael Elins then photographed the heads and married them with images of the kids. He used a Mac equipped with Adobe Photoshop 5.5 and a Wacom tablet that allows him to augment the digital effects with illustration. The entire process takes at least a week. To ensure that the concept was authentic, a futurist from the RAND Corporation and a robot designer from Jet Propulsion Laboratory were consulted.

4. Aibo, the robot dog from Sony, now qualifies as a global fetish. Ten thousand Americans were chosen by lottery to purchase the $2,500 pooch before Christmas, and demand was so exorbitant that Sony sold them again this month (until last Friday). Sadly, there are no additional production plans. But there's always eBay, where you can expect to pay even more.

5. Of course, price is no object when a girl loves a shoe. However, these sandals by Stride Rite are, at $27, very reasonable, Mom.

6. Besides chicken breasts with peanut sauce, vegetable salads and green beans, Loesha Zeviar of GoodFood Catering prepared kid-friendly lunch classics like macaroni and cheese, chocolate cupcakes decorated with flowers (a) and peanut-butter-and-jelly sandwiches. And speaking of jam, G Star Jam Gel (b), $10 at Zitomer (969 Madison Avenue), is a soap for washing out fresh little kids' mouths. Of which there were none.

7. Fresh is the definition of these vintage jeans appliqued by Ellen Hecht, who runs a clothing business called Reciprocity (310-271-5573) when she's not ferrying daughter Chandler to and from assignments.

8. Robotic clones are just the beginning: Kevin Warwick, a professor of cybernetics at the University of Reading, in Britain, had a chip implanted in his body as the first part of an ongoing effort to see how up close and personal we can get with our computers. For nine days, while the implant was in Warwick's arm, his whereabouts, he reports in the February issue of Wired, were monitored by a computer that was able to perform simple tasks like opening doors for him. Within the next two years, Warwick hopes to have another chip implanted that will tap into his central nervous system and enable him to communicate intranervously with his wife, Irena, who, if all goes well, will be outfitted, too.   Peter McQuaid

clean_headline: Style; Maid to Order
abstract:
As someone whose autobiography might be titled ''Too Far to Walk,'' I love the idea of robots. How nice it would be to push a button -- no, my robot will be voice-activated! -- and have a machine write my thank-you notes (the Etiquetti 1320X); find my keys (a Ferretizer); fill out insurance forms (the Toshiba Triplicate Man); argue with Bell Atlantic over my bill (#*@!!! ); make up my mind about what to order at restaurants (le Cuisinateur); tell me what to wear in the morning (the Mombot); get rid of the clothes in my closet that I never wear (the Incinerator II); buy gifts for my friends and relatives (the Santoid); stand in line to have those gifts wrapped (the WaitMate); read the newspaper and provide me with daily synopses (Pentium News Processor); make witty repartee in my place at cocktail parties (the Banteraton); fix my computer, program the VCR and in general be in charge of all the other robotlike appliances in my house (a PowerOmniboss); and then, never flagging, finish writing this article (the Me2). 

 Other people have different fantasies. Senator Bob Kerrey hopes for ''a robot to control the other robots that are controlling my mind.'' Nell Minow, a movie critic, would like ''a robot that turns baser metals (like hangers) into gold and can cope with a last-minute science-fair project, make cookies for a bake sale and always have the right change for pizza day.'' She would also like a robot to go see the Pokemon movie.

abstract: Scientists discover large stone tools in South China dated to about 800,000 years ago, leading scholars to believe that hominids of eastern Asia were as technologically advanced as their counterparts in Africa and Eurasia; new research shows that fish surveyors need not concern themselves with noise when studying fish stocks; scientists in Scotland describe how they used extremely quiet robot submersibles to conduct sonar surveys of herring in North Sea; results are not appreciably different from surveys taken short time later from much noisier boat; astronomers locate two tiny moons of Uranus after 14 years (M)

clean_headline: Surgery, With Help From a Robot
abstract: A robot with three tiny metal arms, operated by a surgeon at a computer console, is performing heart operations that require incisions not much larger than a pencil. Heart surgeons say this sort of operation, still in the testing phase, could transform the profession. Eventually, they say, the doctor will not have to be in the same room, or even the same country, as the patient. Surgeons say that while there is intense interest in doing heart bypass surgery with robots, the methods also could be used for other kinds of microsurgery, like sewing fallopian tubes or working on blood vessels in the brain. Science Times, Page F1.

clean_headline: Next Up: Surgery by Remote Control
abstract: Experimental robot-assisted surgery allows doctors to get deep inside body through tiny incisions, reducing recovery time for procedures as complex as heart surgery; surgeons believe that most heart surgery will eventually be done by robots whose arms are inserted through pencil-sized holes punched in patients' chests; drawings illustrate robotic surgery; photo of Dr Robert E Michler (M)

abstract: IN the 1930's, when the Swiss developmental psychologist Jean Piaget quizzed children to find out if they could tell the difference between living creatures and inanimate objects, he concluded that they defined life by figuring out which objects could move by themselves, without an outside push or pull.

clean_headline: BROOKLYN GUNMAN WOUNDS 3 OFFICERS BEFORE BEING SLAIN
abstract: Deranged man barricaded in his Brooklyn home with arsenal of weapons holds police at bay for tense six-hour siege, then engages in gun battle with police that wounds three officers before police sharpshooters kill him; police say man, Arthur Alalouf, had threatened to kill his family; during siege police had cordoned off house and evacuated neighborhood to prevent civilian casualties, and used robot TV cameras, bullet-proof shields and nonviolent tactics trying to defuse dangerous situation; incident illustrates perils of confrontations with armed, emotionally disturbed people; Mayor Rudolph W Giuliani, at hospital to visit injured officers, calls them heroes and says New York City Police Dept's Emergency Services unit is best in world; photo (M)

clean_headline: WORLD BRIEFING
abstract: EUROPE    NORTHERN IRELAND: OMAGH REMEMBRANCE -- A ceremony commemorated the second anniversary of 29 deaths in a bombing carried out in Omagh by the Irish Republican Army splinter group the Real I.R.A. Prime Ministers Bertie Ahern of Ireland and Tony Blair of Britain pledged continued support for the investigation into the bombing. In March the Irish police commissioner, Pat Byrne, said the police knew those responsible but lacked evidence to bring charges.   Brian Lavery (NYT)    SPAIN: POLICE DETONATE CAR BOMB -- The police used a robot to blow up a car carrying 220 pounds of explosives, which was found abandoned on a highway near Huesca at the foot of the Pyrenees. Police officials suspected that the car bomb had been prepared by the Basque separatist group E.T.A., four of whose members died last week when explosives blew up in their car.   (AP)

clean_headline: February 11-17; Touchdown
abstract: While NASA astronauts installed a modular laboratory at the International Space Station, another little spacecraft that could -- did. NEAR Shoemaker, the first craft to orbit an asteroid, also became the first to land on one when it touched down on Eros after circling it for a year. Somewhat to the surprise of its builders at the Johns Hopkins University Applied Physics Laboratory, the NASA robot craft survived a landing it was not designed to make and was awarded extra time to transmit bonus scientific data to Earth. The first in a series of faster, cheaper and less complicated space missions for NASA, the $223 million NEAR project shows that simpler solutions can be good ones.   Warren E. Leary

clean_headline: New Economy; Why a movie by Spielberg failed to excite a public that was tried of technology.
abstract: AS quietly as a robot drifting into sleep mode, the science fiction fantasy ''A.I.: Artificial Intelligence'' recently slipped from the box office top 10, a mere four weeks after its debut.    The movie's writer and director, Steven Spielberg, and its distributor, Warner Brothers, had probably counted on a better outcome, given Mr. Spielberg's record with such past science fiction hits as ''Close Encounters of the Third Kind'' and, especially, ''E.T.: The Extraterrestrial.'' But the disappointing ticket sales for ''A.I.'' may say something significant about the public's current attitude toward technology.

clean_headline: August 12-18; Higher
abstract: Sometimes the race goes to the slow and fragile. Helios, a solar-powered flying wing built for NASA, broke the altitude record for non-rocket aircraft set 25 years ago. Powered by 14 small electric motors each turning a propeller, Helios climbed above the skies of Hawaii at barely 25 miles per hour to reach 96,500 feet, topping the old mark by more than 11,000 feet. The robot plane, made of lightweight materials covered with a plastic film skin, is a forerunner to craft that one day may soar in the rarefied air of Mars.     Warren E. Leary

clean_headline: Seeking Ancient Life? Ask the Robot Where to Trowel

clean_headline: Yamato Journal; Rover's Just a Robot, but a Great Pal for All That
abstract: Yamato Journal on popularity of Aibo robot toy dogs in Japan, where artificial companionship is taken for granted; photo of young hospital patient playing with Aibo dog, which are actually most popular among lonely elderly people; map (M)

clean_headline: Robots Learn Soccer (and the Game of Life)
abstract: Dr Tucker Balch of Georgia Institute of Technology uses virtual rewards to teach soccer to teams of robots, and results do not support belief that pursuit of individual rewards -- as in capitalism -- encourage people to develop diversity of ideas, points of view, goals and strategies to achieve them; when reward is given only to robot who scores, all members of team that develops go after the ball; when entire team is rewarded on a goal, some robots wind up becoming defenders, others forwards; photo (M)

clean_headline: Tracking of Anthrax Letter Yields Clues
abstract: Investigators are able to track anthrax-laced mail and attempt to reconstruct events that may have led to deaths of Ottilie Lundgren and Kathy T Nguyen only because of rapidly increasing automation of postal service; as mail enters system, bar codes applied to back and front of each piece as it is canceled enable precision tracking; data could allow postal service to identify and publicize mail that passed through Hamilton Township center within specified period of each of four known anthrax mailings; some are pressing for release of data to enable people to avoid exposure to anthrax, while others say such action would only cause alarm (M)

clean_headline: Deep Space 1 Ends Mission, But Triumphs Are Clear
abstract: NASA shuts down Deep Space 1 after it completes its mission; robot spacecraft, used mainly to test technologies for future spacecraft, beamed to Earth clearest, most detailed pictures ever taken of comet when it passed near comet Borrelly (M)

clean_headline: When Nerds Collide: Bots in the Ring
abstract: Fighting robots spawn several television programs, BattleBots, Robotica and Robot Wars, as well as almost 60 live competitions; making robots that fight is irresistable to their builders, some of whom claim it has educational component; provides guilt-free violence that plays on love-hate relationship consumers have with technology; photos (M)

clean_headline: Popular Mechanics
abstract: In this lesson, students learn about the five essential parts of every robot and design their own robots to perform everyday tasks.

clean_headline: Private Sector; Learning to Time the Market And Then, Who Knows?
abstract: Asimo, humanoid robot from Honda Motor, will make history on Feb 14 when it rings bell to open New York Stock Exchange trading day; Asimo, which will be accompanied by Honda chairman-chief executive Richard A Grasso and top Honda executives to note 25th anniversary of Honda's exchange listing; photo (S)

clean_headline: Microsoft Is Putting Its Muscle Behind Web Programming Tools
abstract: Microsoft Corp begins huge campaign to woo millions of computer programmers to use its new generation of Internet software tools; outcome of campaign will partly determine shape of competition in software industry in next several years; competition's focus will be shifting to so-called Web services--clever software that opens door to offering new level of computerized automation and convenience to companies and consumers; Microsoft and rivals, like IBM and Sun Microsystems, are scrambling to supply businesses with these services, which will allow computers to share data across Internet and, when so programmed, handle all kinds of tasks without human intervention; Anders Hejlsberg is chief architect of C# computer language for developers, crucial part of new Microsoft initiative; chairman Bill Gates is scheduled to speak to thousands of software developers in San Francisco, and chief executive Steven A Ballmer will speak to thousands more in Chicago about new software tools; photo (M)

clean_headline: 'Eureka!' Off Gibraltar: A Trove From 1694?
abstract: Team of entrepreneurs and achaeologists working with British government says it has probably found HMS Sussex, British warship that sank in Mediterranean off Gibraltar in 1694, reportedly carrying gold and silver coins now worth up to $4 billion; team's robot has examined large mound rich in cannons, anchors and solidified masses of artifacts and has lifted few to surface; Odyssey Marine Exploration Inc leads project and is negotiating with Britain, which owns wreck, for excavation contract; maps; diagram; photos (M)

clean_headline: An Engine for a New Age of Ocean Research
abstract: Engineers at Webb Research Corp have developed sleek, slow-moving and hardy diving robot, costing $50,000 each, powered by thermal engine, capable of zigzagging through deep ocean water for five years and feeding information to scientists via satellite; diagrams; map (M)

abstract: Interview with Prof Robert J Full, robot maker who patterns his designs on nature; photos (M)

clean_headline: Microsoft, I.B.M. and VeriSign To Cooperate on Web Security
abstract: Microsoft, IBM and VeriSign plan to announce new technical approach that they hope will ensure greater security and thus stimulate commercial development of emerging Internet technology called Web services; Web services is term used to describe clever software that in theory could bring new level of automation and greater productivity to all kinds of online transactions among companies, suppliers and consumers; new, unproven technology--which uses Web to find and share data in electronic databases of companies or individuals--has stirred concerns about data security and personal privacy (M)

abstract: NASA is preparing to launch Contour robot spacecraft atop Delta II rocket from Cape Canaveral Air Force Station in Florida; Contour will make close observations of frozen cores of comets and stand ready to be diverted if Schwassman-Wachmann 3, comet discovered in 1995, passes near Earth; drawings (M)

clean_headline: Cost-Cutting Ax Is Falling Again at ABB
abstract: ABB Ltd chief executive Jurgen Dormann's restructing plan calls for $800 million in cuts, whole division to be sold, rest of company to be shuffled and thousands more jobs to be eliminated; Dormann says company lost $183 million in third quarter because of cost overruns on existing projects and 13 percent fall in new orders; company faces severe threats to its ability to ride out slump in business: rising debts, which reached $5.5 billion in September, and flood of litigation over asbestos liability, 110,000 suits so far; Swiss labor union says it is worried that 18,000 to 20,000 jobs could be lost; Dormann proposes rearranging core businesses into two groups, one for electric-power-related products and one for automation; third division called Group Processes would be scrapped and fourth, including company's oil, gas and petrochemicals, would be sold; photo (M)

abstract: Interview with Jurgen Dormann, chairman and chief executive of ABB Group, on his efforts to put company's balance sheet and financial structure in order; $5.5 billion of its debt is maturing this month; Dorman's plan involves selling profitable divisions, insulating ABB from asbestos-related lawsuits filed against its American subsidiary, Combustion Engineering, and shrinking ABB to 100,000 employees -- down from current 140,000 -- in power technologies and automation; photo (M)

abstract: Aquatrols patents additive for artificial snow which it says allows manufactured snow to form at temperatures above freezing and makes more snow faster; photo; Friendly Robotics patents snow-shoveling robot (M)

clean_headline: Technology Briefing
abstract: E-COMMERCE SYNOPSYS TO BUY NUMERICAL TECHNOLOGIES Synopsys Inc. has agreed to buy Numerical Technologies Inc. for $250 million, or $7 a share, the companies announced yesterday. The acquisition price represents an 89.2 percent premium, based on Numerical's closing price of $3.70 a share on Friday. Shares of Numerical, which is based in San Jose, Calif., closed yesterday at $6.91, up $3.21, or about 87 percent. Chip-making companies use Numerical's software and other applications to produce integrated circuits with patterns that are smaller than the wavelength of light. Numerical's applications are based on phase-shifting and optical correction technologies that compensate for distortions caused by working in such reduced dimensions. Synopsys, which is based in Mountain View, Calif., makes electronic design automation software. The company expects the deal to speed production of sub-wavelength chips as well as reduce costs and manufacturing risk for customers developing power-efficient integrated circuits. The acquisition requires regulatory approval and other closing conditions. The deal is expected to close in the first quarter.   (Dow Jones/AP) COPYRIGHT ACCORD ON DIGITAL SHARING The recording industry and a coalition of technology companies have reached an agreement in principle to oppose any government-required technologies that would prevent consumers from sharing songs and other digital material. The Recording Industry Association of America and two technology groups, the Business Software Alliance and the Computer Systems Policy Project, plan to offer details of the agreement at a news conference today. The groups are expected to back voluntary means to stop piracy. The accord does not include the Motion Picture Association of America, which has supported government action to prevent digital piracy.   (NYT).

She also makes collages and he owns MDF Publishing, a company that produces a monthly newsletter and organizes home technology seminars and events.

clean_headline: More Consumers Reach Out to Touch the Screen

abstract: Japan, world's most rapidly aging nation, experiments with new automated elder care products like 'human washing machine' enjoyed by residents of nursing home in Machida; photos; companies are also marketing items like 'robot suit,' battery-operated pants to help old people get around on their own, and mobile talking camera-robot used by working people to watch elderly parents at home; Japanese sales of domestic robots are now nearly $4 billion a year, and may reach $14 billion in 2010; Philippines and Thailand propose alternative route of allowing in tens of thousands of nurses, notion that falters in face of anti-immigrant sentiment (M)

clean_headline: No Riders: Desert Crossing Is for the Robots Only
abstract: Preliminary tests are set to begin in quest for $1 million being offered by Pentagon to creators of first self-guided vehicle to find its way along programmed course across 200 miles of Mojave Desert; Pentagon is under mandate to turn to unmanned combat vehicles to meet third of its needs by 2015; prize has inspired motley band of computer scientists, artificial intelligence experts and robot lovers to take on challenge of designing ground-hugging, path-finding automated vehicles capable of navigating unaided at average speed of about 20 miles an hour through desert strewn with boulders, trees, brush, potholes and the odd desert animal; entrants include motorcycles, pickups, sports utility vehicles and other all-terrain vehicles, all packed with computers and sensors; favorite is robot Hummer entered by Carnegie Mellon University; photo; map (M)

clean_headline: Robot Vehicle Successfully Navigates Test Course
abstract: Hummer-based robotic vehicle designed by team from Carnegie Mellon University becomes first driverless contestant to navigate 1.36 mile test course in preparation for Defense Department's $1 million robot race; vehicle, named Sandstorm and representing investment of more than $3 million, has generally been considered favored entrant for March 13 between Barstow, Calif, and Las Vegas; Sandstorm's success stands in contrast to test failures by two competitors, vehicle built by team from California Institute of Technology and another one prepared by SciAutonics II, team backed by Southern California aerospace firms (M)

clean_headline: Robots That Build (But Still Won't Do Windows)
abstract: Dr Behrokh Khoshnevis, engineering professor at University of Southern California, has invented robot that may one day build houses and apartment buildings, saving time and money and reducing human costs like injuries; his goal now is to completely construct one-story, 2,000-square-foot home on site in one day, without using human hands; experiment has been financed so far mainly by National Science Foundation and Office of Naval Research; photos (M)

clean_headline: No Winner in a Desert Race for Robots
abstract: None of 15 robot vehicles completes 142-mile race in Mojave Desert sponsored by Defense Advanced Research Projects Agency; vehicles had to traverse course without human intervention, for $1 million prize; top qualifier, Carnegie Mellon University's Sandstorm, which cost $3 million and was built with help of Boeing and Intel, was out of race within first hour after covering 7.4 miles; within four hours of start, all vehicles had crashed or been immobilized; Darpa spent $13 million on competition, but contestants invested as much as $65 million; photo (M)

clean_headline: Now, a Robot That Toots Its Own Horn
abstract: Toyota unveils humanoid robot that walks, waves its arms, bows and plays trumpet; hopes to build series of such autonomous machines over next two decades to serve as personal assistants, aides for the elderly and laborers; also displays rolling robot and shows film of pair of robotic legs that its engineers designed to carry disabled person; photo (M)

clean_headline: Holly Laningham, George Mattson
abstract: Holly Melissa Laningham, a daughter of Joan and Glen Laningham of Huntington Beach, Calif., was married yesterday evening in Miami Beach to George Nathaniel Mattson, a son of Helen and Gregory Mattson of Bethesda, Md. The Rev. Dr. Karl R. Rodig, a Roman Catholic priest, officiated at the Vanderbilt mansion on Fisher Island.     Mrs. Mattson, 33, was until 1999 a runway model in Rome with Elite Model Management. She also worked as an actor in commercials and films. She had a lead role in ''Fashionably L.A.,'' an independent film comedy about the business of modeling. She graduated from San Diego State University. Her father retired as the vice president for human resources at General Automation, a maker of computers that was then in Anaheim, Calif.

clean_headline: Robo-Cars Make Cruise Control So Last Century
abstract: Ever-increasing computerization of automobile and evolution of sensory technology are shifting control over braking, steering, parking and other aspects of driving from the human behind the wheel to car itself, and trend towards self-driving car is expected to continue; most such technology begins in luxury cars, where owners more easily absorb costs, but not always; increasing complexity has ramifications for quality because automobiles have more physical parts, and now also software, that can fail; is among reasons for rising numbers of vehicle recalls; automakers say potential safety benefits offset such concerns; drawing (M)

clean_headline: Cognitive Rascal in the Amorous Swamp: A Robot Battles Spam
abstract: George Johnson essay about ongoing battle with spam email personifies SpamProbe software as his beautiful Helen; explains how program uses Bayesian inference to learn ways to identify unwanted email and recognize legitimate messages; limits of SpamProbe discussed; drawing (M)

clean_headline: What Good Is a Hookworm-Free Robot if It Can't Lick Your Face?
abstract: Animals carry disease, but Centers for Disease Control and Prevention points out that catching anything serious from family pet is unlikely unless family member has compromised immune system; zoonosis, which are illnesses that jump from animals to people, are of increasing concern, but are infrequent; Aibo, Sony's robot dog, is one way to avoid germs and bacteria, but mechanical canine lacks some warmth; cartoon (M)

clean_headline: NASA Weighs Robot Mission To Maintain Telescope
abstract: NASA administrator Sean O'Keefe, four and half months after he seemed to doom Hubble Space Telescope by ending maintenance missions by space shuttle, brings brighter news to astronomers by announcing that NASA is seeking proposals for robotic mission to extend Hubble's life; decision is compromise between Hubble supporters who would like O'Keefe to reinstate shuttle mission and initial prospect that an otherwise still useful telescope will be able to operate for about three more years before failure of gyroscopes would render it unable to point at stars; O'Keefe gives his views in speech to American Astronomical Society, Denver (M)

clean_headline: COMPANY BRIEFS
abstract: Veritas Software Corp., Mountain View, Calif., a leading maker of data storage software, agreed to buy closely held Invio Software Inc., Los Altos, Calif., for $35 million to gain some automation technology.    Penn Virginia Resource Partners, Radnor, Pa., manager of coal properties, acquired a 50 percent interest in a joint venture from an affiliate of Massey Energy Co., Richmond, Va., for $28.5 million.

clean_headline: We, Robots
abstract: Editorial compares new film I, Robot to 1950 Isaac Asimov book of same name; holds that Asimov's robots are perfectly logical, and therefore all problems are caused by humans, who are unaware that their intentions and emotions run counter to logic; contends that it is no wonder Hollywood prefers simply to fear robots, as it does in film; maintains that it is vastly easier and more thrilling than introspection

clean_headline: COMPANY BRIEFS
abstract: ARM Holdings Plc, Cambridge, England, a designer of semiconductors, said it had agreed to buy Axys Design Automation Inc., Irvine, Calif., a software maker. Terms were not disclosed.    General Dynamics Corp., Falls Church, Va., the defense contractor, said it had won a contract from the Army to make devices to detect biological warfare agents. The contract could be worth as much as $750 million through 2009.

clean_headline: Can a Robot Save Hubble? More Scientists Think So
abstract: Sean O'Keefe, National Aeronautics and Space Administration administrator, reports that although agency has decided to stop sending astronauts to repair and maintain Hubble telescope, plans to continue to service telescope through robotic spacecraft are moving forward; original decision to allow Hubble to become inactive was met with intense criticism; many scientists hold that Hubble has provided some of most valuable space images to date; NASA is currently considering about six proposals for robotic servicing missions and says plan should be in place by fall; enthusiasm for robotic mission is growing and funding now comes under new space exploration initiatives instituted by Bush administration; drawing (L)

clean_headline: Attacks on Windows PC's Grew in First Half of 2004
abstract: Symantec survey of Internet vulnerabilities finds sharp jump in attacks on Windows-based personal computers in 2004 first half, along with marked rise in commercially motivated threats; warns of significant rise in number of 'bot,' or robot, networks, arrays of interconnected personal computers that have been compromised to inject large volumes of viruses, worms, spyware or spam into Internet; creators of bot networks sell access to them to commercial spammers and others who wish to send data anonymously (M)

clean_headline: The Art of the Takedown, by Car, Fist or Robot
abstract: Charles Herold Game Theory column reviews Burnout 3: Takedown, WWE: Day of Reckoning and Astro Boy: Omega Factor; photos (M)

clean_headline: The Live Wire At Stevens Tech
abstract: Profile of and interview with Harold Raveche, president of Stevens Institute in Hoboken, NJ; Raveche notes that Stevens, in early 1970's, was first university to require students to have computers; Stevens connected all its dormitories and classrooms to computer network within university in mid-1980's, far earlier than most schools and campus was completely rewired to support gigabit networking in 1998; Stevens hopes to have robot vehicles roaming campus by next year, finding and then enhancing weak spots in school's wireless network; photos (M)

clean_headline: Deeper Trouble Vexing E.D.S. and Other Data Services
abstract: $400 billion-a-year computer services business is not in great shape; analysts say main challenges are backlog of problem contracts, increasing need to substitute automation for labor in managing corporate data centers, and difficulty of making transition from low-margin outsourcing services to areas of higher profitability; say computer services companies must shift steadily from managing armies of workers in computing engine rooms of corporations to offering customized packages of technology services for tasks; fast-emerging Indian outsourcing firms like Infosys, Wipro and Satyam are posing formidable challenges to older companies like Electronic Data Systems; financial problems at Electronic Data Systems discussed; graph (Market Place column) (M)

clean_headline: A Submersible Robot Dives for Steamship Gold
abstract: Odyssey Marine Exploration Inc unloads cargo found during recent salvage dive from wreck of Republic, steamer that sank during hurricane off coast of Georgia in 1865; artifacts, including bottles and valuable gold coins, described; technology uses suction cups to reduce unnecessary marring of objects recovered; digital photographic montage of wreckage aids in salvage of treasure; photos;, map (M)

clean_headline: Making the Outdoors a Bit Greater
abstract: <!--.anchor { font-family: Arial, Helvetica, sans-serif; font-size: 12px; line-height: 13px; color: #333333}.title { font-family: Arial, Helvetica, sans-serif; font-size: 18px; line-height: 25px; color: #333333}.subhead { font-family: Arial, Helvetica, sans-serif; font-size: 13px; line-height: 15px; color: #333333}--> function pop_me_up(pURL, features) { new_window = window.open(pURL, "popup_window", features); new_window.focus(); } Recreational Tools to Get the Most Out of Mother NaturePARALLAX VIEWWhen Bushnell (bushnell.com) decided to marry a 2-megapixel digital camera with its famous binoculars, it went one step beyond the competition. In most binocams, the camera uses its lens rather than the binoculars' for zeroing in on subjects. That can lead to framing errors. In Bushnell's $600 Instant Replay, the camera is integrated into the binoculars. The camera can also record short low-resolution videos.MULTITASKERThe TotalFit from Bell, the maker of protective helmets and other safety gear and accessories, has a double identity. Mounted on a bicycle, it is a speedometer, but clip it to your belt and it becomes a pedometer. It keeps track of the calories you've burned during your walk, run or ride. The $20 device, available at sports and toy retailers and at sites like fogdog.com, has an easy-to-read display and can be set to English or metric units.TIME CHECKThe Casio Atomic Solar G-Shock (GW700A-1V) wristwatch gets its power from the sun and its accuracy from the government. The $100 watch's solar cells convert light into electrical energy. Several times a day it receives a wireless signal from an atomic clock operated by the National Institute of Standards and Technology in Colorado, keeping it calibrated with official government time. It is shock and water resistant, it has a calendar feature, and it can handle up to four daily alarms and one snooze alarm. More information is at casio.com.DAS BOTWhy not put a robot to work keeping the swimming pool sparkling while you relax in the chaise? The Aquabot Bravo senses a pool's design, taking account of odd areas like stairs. It then calculates the most efficient cleaning pattern. Aqua Products, its maker, says it can scrub and vacuum a pool clean in an hour. The Aquabot Bravo ($1,400; aquaservicedist.com) can be ordered from dealers recommended by Aqua Products (800-221-1750Ã).TRACK MATEWireless heart rate monitors have long been used by runners to gauge athletic performance. But most of them don't also keep track of distance. The Polar S625X running computer ($350; polarusa.com) comes with a wireless distance sensor that attaches to the runner's shoes. The device also measures elevation changes during a run and, with optional accessories, can double as a bicycle computer. HEAR, HEARBy amplifying sounds, the Brunton Nature Ear II ($280; brunton.com) does for bird watchers' ears what binoculars do for their eyes. The device concentrates on frequencies commonly associated with birdcalls, thus minimizing other sounds. SKY KINGThe Meade ETX-90AT Astro Telescope with Autostar Computer Controller requires little knowledge of astronomy to find stars and other night sky objects. Unlike earlier computer-controlled telescopes, the ETX-90AT ($600; meade.com) even automatically orients and levels itself. Its database contains more than 30,000 objects.

clean_headline: A Robot for the Masses
abstract: Francisco Goldman article on design and development of Robosapien, developed by robotic physicist Mark Tilden for WowWee; photos (L)

clean_headline: ROCKWELL CHIEF TO ADD CHAIRMAN'S POSITION
abstract: Rockwell Automation says its president and chief executive, Keith Nosbusch, will take additional position of chairman in February, replacing Don Davis, who is retiring (S)

clean_headline: In Kingdom of Cockroaches, Leaders Are Made, Not Born
abstract: Dr Jose Halloy, Free University of Brussels senior research scientist working on behavior project along with several institutions, explains current research into ways of influencing animal behavior; describes robot roaches used in ongoing study on collective behavior; photo (S)

clean_headline: Best Way to Skip a Stone, The
abstract: Clive Thompson article, in review of year's notable ideas and developments, describes work by French scientists who discovered that best way to skip a stone is to throw it at angle of precisely 10 degrees to the water; problem was solved with aid of robot that could whip metal disks at tank of water with utter precision; scientists admit that there is no practical use for knowledge; drawings (M)

clean_headline: Honda's Robot Advances, but to Where?
abstract: Honda Motor Co introduces new version of its humanoid robot that can jog, find its way around obstacles and respond to human touch; developers of robot called Asimo say new model is significant advance over earlier versions; Honda has not made much progress in determining just what robot might be used for; photo (M)

clean_headline: Money to Fix Space Telescope May Be Cut by White House
abstract: NASA is one of few agencies to get budget increase for fiscal 2006, five percent rise to $16.2 billion, but Bush administration officials say more than $1 billion needed for mission to repair Hubble Space Telescope will not be part of package; NASA Administrator Sean O'Keefe's Jan 2004 decision on grounds of safety not to allow shuttle repair mission to orbiting observatory resulted in wave of criticism and NASA began developing plan to use robot device for repairs (M)

clean_headline: The New Boss
abstract: Matt Bai article profiles Andrew Stern, president of Service Employees International Union, which represents janitors, health aides and other service workers; while old-line industrial unions are in decline, Stern has been organizing low-wage workers, most of whom have never belonged to union, at torrid pace to point where SEIU is largest and fastest-growing trade union in North America; Stern has scolded labor bosses for failing to adapt to new world in which automation and globalization have savaged organized labor; threatens to bolt AFL-CIO if other bosses do not embrace reform, beginning with plan to merge its 58 unions into 20 for purpose of consolidating power; intends to create new, more dynamic kind of movement around 21st-century workers; critics accuse him of being dictatorial; career highlights; photos (L)

clean_headline: QUOTATION OF THE DAY
abstract: ''They don't get hungry. They're not afraid. They don't forget their orders. They don't care if the guy next to them has just been shot.''    GORDON JOHNSON, of the Pentagon's Joint Forces Command, on robot soldiers. [A1]

clean_headline: New Model Army SoldierRolls Closer to Battle
abstract: Second article in series Arsenal of the Future discusses robots as crucial part of Army's plans for 21st-century fighting force; $127-billion Future Combat Systems project, biggest military contract in US history, will help drive up Pentagon budget almost 20 percent, from requested $419.3 billion next year to $502.3 billion in 2010, excluding war costs; military planners say robot soldiers will think, see and react increasingly like humans, taking many shapes and gaining autonomy as technology advances; photos; armed version of bomb-disposal robot will be in Baghdad this April, capable of firing 1,000 rounds a minute as commanded by soldier with laptop; even supporters like Rodney A Broks of MIT warn that real world is not Hollywood; ultimate ideal of unmanned warfare is combat without casualties but, short of that, goal is to give difficult, dull or dangerous missions to robots, conserving American minds and protecting American bodies in battle (M)

clean_headline: Corrections
abstract: Correction of Feb 16 article on military's efforts to build robot soldiers regarding quotation from book about robots

clean_headline: What will a $200,000 robot do in North Dakota?
A Robot for the Home, Brains Not Included
abstract: Robotics has always been an esoteric and expensive pursuit, with few avenues for the home dabbler to create a robot that can venture around the house or out into the world. The White Box Robotics Model 914 PC-BOT, however, aims to change that.    The PC-BOT ($1,199, whiteboxrobotics.com) is essentially a PC case on wheels. The chassis is pressed steel, and the basic PC-BOT includes two 12-volt rechargeable batteries as well as a Webcam, microphone and object-avoidance sensors. It has rubber drive wheels with independent suspension.

clean_headline: Now There Are Many: Robots That Reproduce
abstract: Perhaps one day scientists will turn the Michael Crichton nightmare of ''Prey'' into reality and unleash tiny robots that multiply like rabbits and overwhelm biological life into oblivion.    But for now, what passes for a state-of-the-art, self-reproducing robot looks like a stack of blocks. Actually, it is a stack of blocks, one with the ability to pick up other blocks and clone itself into a second identical stack.

clean_headline: Anna Lumelsky and Joshua Goodman
abstract: Anna Esther Lumelsky, the daughter of Dr. Nadya Lumelsky and Dr. Vladimir Lumelsky of Washington, is to be married today in Woodcliff Lake, N.J., to Joshua Samuel Goodman, the son of Judith and Stephen M. Goodman of White Plains. Rabbi Gordon Tucker is to perform the ceremony at Temple Emanuel of Pascack Valley.    Ms. Lumelsky, 26, will keep her name. She is a law clerk for Judge Patti B. Saris of the Federal District Court in Boston. In September the bride is to become a clerk for Judge Julio M. Fuentes of the United States Court of Appeals for the Third Circuit in Newark. She graduated magna cum laude from Harvard, where she received her law degree cum laude. Her father retired as a professor in the computer science, mathematics and engineering departments at the University of Wisconsin and is now a lead scientist on space robotics and intelligent automation projects at the Goddard Space Center in Greenbelt, Md., part of NASA. Her mother is a research scientist at the National Institute of Diabetes and Digestive and Kidney Diseases in Bethesda, conducting stem cell research.

clean_headline: A Better Robot, With Help From Roaches
abstract: Garnet Hertz, a graduate student at the University of California, Irvine has given a roach a car.     The idea, he says, is to take a novel approach to the problem of robotic navigation. In the past, robots have not been particularly adroit; getting from Point A to Point B can be arduous, and navigation systems cumbersome and complex.

clean_headline: New York and Southampton
abstract: TODAY Sephora is scheduled to open a 5,800-square-foot store in Union Square where shoppers can try out salon-quality blow dryers at the chain's first Hair Style Bar; browse travel-size skincare and color products; and taste lickable cosmetics. In celebration the company is giving a beauty block party on the site of the Greenmarket from 3:30 to 8 p.m. Passers-by can get makeovers or shaving lessons, pick up samples and enjoy a glass of punch or a tuft of cotton candy. At 45 East 17th Street, (212)995-8833.    Giant Robot New York, a shop-cum-art gallery from the Asian pop culture magazine and retailer Giant Robot, will open in the East Village on Saturday. The opening installation features the work of the Japanese sculptor Eishi Takaoka, whose carved wooden heads are mounted on tiny medicine bottles. At 437 East Ninth Street, (212)674-4769.

clean_headline: Cisco's Balance of Automation And Workers
abstract: The technology created by Silicon Valley workers may itself be partly responsible for slowing the rate of employment growth here. More and more, companies are automating functions once performed by people.    Cisco Systems, for instance, uses Internet-based programs to automate certain pieces of its human resources, finance, customer service and support operations. Among other things, this means that when a new employee is hired, the employee enrolls online for benefits rather than meeting with a human resources manager.

clean_headline: I, Roommate: The Robot Housekeeper Arrives
abstract: WHEN my home robot arrived last month, its smiling inventors removed it from its box and laid it on its back on my living room floor. They leaned over and spoke to it, as one might to a sleeping child.      It straightened, let out a little beep, lighted up, looked left and right, and then, amazingly, stood and faced me.      I said, ''Nuvo, how are you?''

clean_headline: Hardly cuddly, but Nuvo the robot can be your buddy
Angelo Cintron and David Areford
abstract: Angelo Manuel Cintron and David Sheridan Areford were married on Friday at City Hall in Cambridge, Mass. E. Denise Simmons, a justice of the peace, officiated.    Mr. Cintron (above, left) is 49. He works in Boston for the management services unit of Pitney Bowes, the postage meter company, working off-site as a supervisor of a client's supply department. He graduated from Kutztown University in Pennsylvania. He is a son of Margarita Cintron of Allentown, Pa., and the late Angel Cintron. His mother, who is retired, assembled circuit boards and other components in Allentown for the electronics manufacturing division of Asea Brown Boveri, the Swiss electrical and automation engineering company. His father was a supervisor at the Spinoza Construction Company, also in Allentown.

clean_headline: QUOTATION OF THE DAY
abstract: ''Automation always looks good on paper. Sometimes you need real people.''    VERONICA STEVENSON, president of a union local representing baggage handlers, on the failure of an automated baggage system at Denver's airport. [A1]

clean_headline: Oracle to Pay $5.85 Billion for Siebel
abstract: Oracle Corp will pay $5.85 billion to acquire Siebel Systems, its largest competitor in market for sales automation programs; deal will bolster Oracle's effort to topple its German rival SAP as leading seller of business applications software for big corporations; Oracle's chief executive Lawrence J Ellison comments; photo (M)

clean_headline: Robot vehicles line up for race in the desert
At Dartmouth, a Remote-Controlled Robot
abstract: For a steerable piece of dust, look somewhere at Dartmouth College.     Researchers there have built what they say is the world's smallest untethered, controllable robot. When placed on a penny, it looks like a mole on the side of Lincoln's chin, measuring a hundredth of an inch by one four-hundredth of an inch.

clean_headline: When a Garage Goes Wrong
abstract: WHEN will the killer robot garage strike again?      The motive is unclear. The weapon is a mystery. But what is certain is that what is being called the country's first fully automated parking garage has already claimed two victims, most recently on Oct. 16, when the $12 million garage sent a Jeep Wrangler plummeting four stories to its demise.

clean_headline: The Strange Case of the Spoofer Captured by a Spoof
abstract: Actor and satirist Chris Elliott has agreed to financial settlement after discovering that Boilerplate, robot character in his debut novel The Shroud of the Thwacker, is creation of illustrator and graphic novelist Paul Guinan; Guinan and his wife, Anina Bennett, wrote Heartbreakers Meet Boilerplate, which was published in July; Elliott says he knew Boilerplate was some kind of spoof but thought it was 19th-century spoof, not post-modern, post-dated parody of a hoax; photos (M)

clean_headline: Robot Jockeys
abstract: Robert Mackey article, in review of year's notable ideas, on use of robots instead of children as jockeys in camel races in Qatar and United Arab Emirates; move comes after years of pressure from human rights groups and Western governments to enforce bans on child jockeys; rather than opt for heavier adults, owners asked robotics firms for help; photo (M)

clean_headline: Not a Speedy Floor Swabber, Maybe, but It Works Without Complaint
abstract: Four hundred dollars for a mop? At that price you'd expect it to clean by itself -- and it does. It's the Scooba, and it's a robotic wet mop from iRobot, makers of the Roomba automated vacuum cleaner.     Put a pint of cleaning solution and water in Scooba's tank and the 10-pound robot will roam a room of up to 200 square feet, spraying out cleaner, scrubbing with a 1,400-r.p.m. brush, then sucking up the dirty water into a separate tank. The dirty water is emptied into the sink the old-fashioned way, by hand. Under four inches tall, the Scooba will even get under your couch. In a test on a kitchen floor measuring roughly six feet by nine feet, a cleaning took about an hour.

clean_headline: This Neighbor Is Into Heavy Metal
abstract: STAN the robot is the all-seeing, all-knowing sentinel of Robot Village. He stands on the sidewalk of West 81st Street, five feet tall, his eyes made from what appear to be toy spaceship hatches, his pale gray limbs resembling battle cruisers from ''Star Wars.'' He does not walk or talk, but he does hold fliers: there are more robots within, they say, and Stan's escape-hatch eyes implore passers-by to come have a look.     Once inside, the curious will confront David Greenbaum, who has turned this small below-ground storefront into a robot wonderland. Since the shop opened last April, Mr. Greenbaum has certainly caught the notice of children, parents and robot enthusiasts from the Upper West Side and beyond, offering a cavalcade of robot people, dogs and dinosaurs.

clean_headline: NASA Mission to Explore Solar System's Edge
abstract: NASA is scheduled to launch New Horizons spacecraft on Jan 17; craft will journey to and get first close-up look at Pluto; spacecraft will reach Jupiter in just 13 months, where gravity assist will speed it on its way to Pluto in so-called Kuiper Belt for arrival in 2015; compact robot craft will be in hibernation for most of flight to conserve energy; once New Horizons completes its study of ice dwarf Pluto and its moons, it will be redirected toward other Kuiper Belt objects; photo; diagrams (M)

clean_headline: For Sony's Robotic Aibo, It's the Last Year of the Dog
abstract: There was sad news last week for enthusiasts of the Aibo Entertainment Robot from Sony: the doglike machine, which walks, barks and recognizes speech, is being put to sleep, the company said.      The Aibo, which was introduced in 1999, is being discontinued as part of Sony's move to improve its financial position. Last September the company announced that it would eliminate or scale back 15 product categories. In addition to Aibo, Sony has also stopped selling its Qualia line of high-end televisions and audio equipment, plasma TV's and car stereos in Japan, said Rick Clancy, a senior vice president at Sony Electronics.

clean_headline: If Robots Ever Get Too Smart, He'll Know How to Stop Them
abstract: Article on Dr Daniel H Wilson book How to Survive a Robot Uprising, which is humorous but comprehensive look at robots and robotics; various sections of book described; drawings; photo (M)

clean_headline: Scaling Great Heights for Very Different Causes
abstract: Mark Ecko's video game Getting Up: Contents Under Pressure follows graffiti artist Trane's goal to cover city with urban art; player must put graffiti in hard-to-reach places and avoid obstacles such as moving trains, competing artists and security guards; game developed by the Collective and published by Atari; costs $50; Chibi-Robo developed by Skip Ltd and published by Nintendo allows user to control house-cleaning robot; Chibi-Robo must also solve problems of other toys in household; costs $50; photos (M)

clean_headline: Corrections
abstract: Because of an editing error, an article in Science Times on Tuesday about the new book ''How to Survive a Robot Uprising'' misidentified a work in which robots rise up against humanity. It is the 2004 movie ''I, Robot.'' (In the short-story collection by Isaac Asimov with the same title, on which the film is loosely based, there is no uprising.)

clean_headline: Letters
abstract: Readers respond to Science Times articles with thoughts on mother-daughter communication, psychotherapy, robot invasions and more.

clean_headline: Laptops in Patrol Cars Speed Up the Process
abstract: Article on new laptop computer, scanner and ticket printer system called Total Enforcement, designed by former Garden City Police Officer Al Perez and made by Total Computer Group of Melville, NY; police departments on Long Island and Westchester County are adopting system or testing ones from other vendors; police say automation not only steps up productivity and reduces clerical errors, but enables them to share information with other forces; photos (M)

clean_headline: The Art of Building a Robot to Love
abstract: Scientists hold conference in Salt Lake City on human-robot interaction; drawing (M)

clean_headline: Software Firm Bluespec Gets $4.5 Million in Funds
abstract: Bluespec has received $4.5 million in its second round of funding from Atlas Ventures and North Bridge Venture. To date, Bluespec, which makes electronic design automation tool sets, has received a total of $13 million in funding from Atlas Venture and North Bridge Venture Partners.

clean_headline: Student's Prize Is a Trip Into Immigration Limbo
abstract: A small, troubled high school in East Harlem seemed an unlikely place to find students for a nationwide robot-building contest, but when a neighborhood after-school program started a team last winter, 19 students signed up. One was Amadou Ly, a senior who had been fending for himself since he was 14.     The project had only one computer and no real work space. Engineering advice came from an elevator mechanic and a machinist's son without a college degree. But in an upset that astonished its sponsors, the rookie team from East Harlem won the regional competition last month, beating rivals from elite schools like Stuyvesant in Manhattan and the Bronx High School of Science for a chance to compete in the national robotics finals in Atlanta that begins tomorrow.

clean_headline: Another Robot Vehicle Contest Is Planned
abstract: The Pentagon said that a third Grand Challenge competition would be held to foster research and development into advanced robot vehicles.

clean_headline: Another Robot Vehicle Contest Is Planned
abstract: Pentagon announces third Grand Challenge competition aimed at encouraging research and development into advanced robot vehicles; challenge will take place in mock urban area this year instead of desert setting of past competitions; first prize will be $2 million (M)

clean_headline: S.E.C. Opens Investigation into Brooks Automation
abstract: Brooks Automation, a maker of equipment and software used to make microchips, said Friday that the Securities and Exchange Commission had opened an informal inquiry into its stock option practices.

clean_headline: Ametek Buys PennEngineering
abstract: Ametek on Monday bought PennEngineering Motion Technologies for about $64 million in cash. Penn makes highly engineered motors for use in the data storage, medical, electronic equipment, factory automation and aviation markets. It has annual sales of around $55 million.

clean_headline: From X's and O's to I.T.
abstract: IDG founder and chairman Pat McGovern The Boss column on building computer at age 15, which won science fair competition and resulted in scholarship to Massachusetts Institute of Technology, landing job as technical editor for Computers and Automation magazine and development of national computer systems customer database; photo (M)

clean_headline: U.S. Expands Its Scrutiny of Stock Grants
abstract: Juniper Networks, F5 Networks, Brooks Automation and Openwave Systems said they had been contacted by prosecutors or regulators.

clean_headline: Free Your Inner Disney With a Video Editing Tool That Can Animate Your Home Movies

Aspiring animators on a budget may want to take a look at the latest version of Adobe's video editing program, Adobe Premiere Elements 3.0.     The software, announced this week and arriving in stores soon, includes a stop-motion video capture function that lets you flow a series of single shots together into one big moving picture. When snapping individual frames from a connected Webcam, for example, the program provides you with a somewhat transparent image of your last shot on the screen so you can better judge the movements of the action figure, clay model or whatever else you're animating.

To donate a gently used Chicco Rock’n’Roll Eggplant or a Little Tikes Classic Whale Teeter-Totter (should you have one) — or to watch a robot dismantle a cheerful yellow duck — go to glform.com.[?][?][?] ALIX BROWNE.

clean_headline: Upside-down Demolition
abstract: The Japanese are masters of innovative ways of knocking down buildings. Previous demolition techniques include giant fire-breathing lizard (Godzilla), giant laser-beam-breathing robot lizard (Mechagodzilla) and giant moth (Mothra), all of which destroyed vast swaths of Tokyo. Now the Kajima Corporation, a Tokyo-based construction, architecture and engineering firm, has come up with a slightly less exciting but no less inventive approach: it recently tore down two of its office buildings from the bottom up, one floor at a time. A time-lapse video shows one of the buildings, supported by hot pink struts, seemingly disappearing, level by level, into the ground.

abstract: Restaurants are experimenting with automation. But in the kitchen, human labor is hard to replace.

clean_headline: OpenAI Details Plans for Becoming a For-Profit Company
abstract: OpenAI said it would be restructured as a public benefit corporation, and that the nonprofit that controls the organization would receive shares in the new company.
